cases.title: Thirty Down at the Stadium
cases.presentation: A structural collapse at a stadium exit leaves roughly 30 casualties. You are the designated triage officer. Some victims are screaming, several lie motionless, and dozens of others mill around dazed.
cases.question: What is your first triage action?
options: A: Begin detailed head-to-toe exams on the nearest patient.
B: Global sort by voice: ask those who can to walk to a designated area, then ask the rest to wave — assessing the still and obviously bleeding first.
C: Start CPR on the first pulseless casualty.
D: Transport the loudest patient first.
cases.explanation: SALT starts with global sorting: walkers are prioritized last, wavers second, and the still or obviously hemorrhaging first. This sorts 30 casualties in seconds and directs individual assessment where it matters most.
cases.clinicalPearl: Let your voice triage the crowd before your hands triage a patient.

cases.title: The Apneic Adult and the Apneic Child
cases.presentation: During your assessment pass you find two apneic casualties near each other: a middle-aged man and a 6-year-old girl. You open each airway with positioning. The man remains apneic. After the girl's airway is opened you consider further action.
cases.question: Per SALT, what is correct?
options: B: The man is Dead (black); for the child, consider two rescue breaths — if breathing resumes she is triaged as living (likely Immediate); if not, she is Dead.
D: Tag both Immediate and move on.
cases.explanation: In mass-casualty triage, an apneic adult after airway opening is Dead — CPR would consume many rescuers for one arrest. Children get consideration of two rescue breaths because pediatric arrest is typically hypoxic and may reverse with ventilation alone.
cases.clinicalPearl: SALT's only 'CPR' is two breaths for a child — the highest-yield seconds in pediatric triage.

cases.title: Gray, Not Black
cases.presentation: A casualty has an open skull injury with exposed brain tissue, agonal-pattern breathing, and no radial pulse. Your only transport is 40 minutes out, and three Immediate patients await treatment. Later, two helicopters and a surgical team arrive.
cases.question: How is this casualty triaged initially, and what happens when resources improve?
options: B: Expectant (gray) initially — comfort care, not abandonment; on resource arrival, re-triage and potentially upgrade to Immediate.
cases.explanation: Survival is unlikely with the resources initially available, so the casualty is Expectant with comfort care. Expectant is resource-relative: when helicopters and a surgical team arrive, re-triage may upgrade the patient to Immediate.
cases.clinicalPearl: Expectant is a statement about your resources, not a verdict on the patient — re-triage when the math changes.
